reviews.comment: LOVE MAZZONE. Really cares about the Constitution, and it comes through in his lectures. It's pretty old school - cold calling, no laptops - but still very engaging because of the topic matter. Mazzone is great and also pretty funny.

Hands down the best. He's clearly passionate about the constitution and made the class enjoyable with his humor despite the cold calling. Get ready to read a lot but definitely worth it as you'll walk out with a really good understanding of the subject.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Participation matters--Amazing lectures

reviews.comment: Professor Mazzone is one of my favorite professors so far. He does an amazing job of playing both sides of the political spectrum to make you think about your answers. He does old school cold calling where if you get called on you'll be talking about the entire case not just a question or two so be prepared.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Get ready to read--Participation matters

reviews.comment: Mazzone assigns a lot of reading and can be tough on people in class. He does not make his classes easy to blow off. But he's hilarious and a real expert in his field. He's incredibly intelligent and you will be lucky to take his courses. You will grow and learn from it.

Participate often and pay attention to the articles in particular. Mastering a smaller amount of the cases and articles seemed to be more rewarding than having a mediocre understanding of everything.  He's a quality professor and knows his stuff. This class is definitely worthwhile. If you liked con law with him, you'll like this too.

Epic reading load. Brace yourselves to read 2000 pgs. The lectures were fun at times, and he's got a quirky sense of humor. He definitely needs to cut down on calling someone, then just PAINSTAKINGLY waiting, and waiting, and waiting even though it's clear as day the student has no idea what to say. Dude, we all get it - she didn't read.

Mazzone has a very unique class compared to other 1L courses. You are not allowed to use a computer. However, this is great for class discussion and participation. He loves that. He can come off as condescending, but is really a sweet man. He grades very fairly. Even though he warns against supplements, many As & Bs use Chemerinsky's successfully.
Professor Pahre is awesome and super chill I love her so much. This is a great class you learn so much and it is wonderful.

Lectures were relevant to daily life and she often included funny clips as part of the modules. Very friendly and understanding, especially during such a hard semester. Class has 6 quizzes that you could take up to three times and a 5 page paper. As long as you don't procrastinate doing the paper, this class is minimally stressful.

Prof. Pahre is just lovely! Lectures are always very engaging and especially articulate. Class covers a lot of ground, but the pacing was just right. 4 online quizzes (1 low score dropped), participation in "law labs," and a short final paper were the only requirements. Highly recommend the course!

Lectures are very interesting, but can run fast at times. She makes good use of videos to support the lectures. She is very knowledgeable about the law, and is always willing to answer student questions. Four midterms are online (open-note) and final is a relatively easy, five-page paper. Papers are graded hard, so make sure to make time for those.

Queen. Possibly one of the most articulate lecturers I've ever experienced. She will never leave you questioning what she means to say. Very clear, confident, kind. Brought clementines for the whole class one day when the flu was going around. She does tackle some dark topics in law, but breaks it up with funny stories too. Love her.

One of the best professors I've had. You did not need to attend lectures, but they were really good so many students attended. She is a true expert in the field and really inspires students to pursue the law. There were 2 extra credit papers.

She is an excellent teacher. No matter your view on the American legal system, it will interest you. She uses a lot of real life examples, often examples that are funny or bizarre. There were two papers (mid-term and final) and five online quizzes. Read the book and you should be fine. ATTENDANCE IS REQUIRED. NO FINAL EXAM.

Professor Pahre always demystified law. She challenged students to think about why law is the way it is. Her "war stories" provided a realistic view of what lawyers actually do each day. What you get out of this class if up to you: attend lecture and read everything that is assigned, and you'll learn A LOT; otherwise, it's just very laid back.

Phenomenal professor, she truly cares about her students and tailors her lectures to keeping class interesting. Very easy class with limited quizzes, midterms that the TA's give a lot of direction on if you ask, and extra credit to help you out. Makes law relevant for even those not wanting a career in it. TAKE THIS CLASS you will not regret it.

She shows SO MANY VIDEOS in the class, and talks about current issues that intertwine with law. It's a fairly easy class, the quizzes are online - one is dropped. Good class to get a general understanding of law

She is my favorite professor by far. Not only does she care about her students but she tries to make the material as enjoyable as possible. She even brought a class of 200 apple cider donuts FRESHLY baked from curtis apple orchard. She goes out of her way to make her students feel special and she truly does a great job of teaching.

Readings are assigned and "required" (I think) but you can easily get away without reading a single page. Really the only mandatory part of the class is attendance (when she remembers to pass around the sign-in sheet) and the papers, which you should take seriously. Makes the constitution digestible and interesting for students.
Great prof!
AMAZING. NICE. EASY. INTERESTING. THE ONLY ISSUE WITH THE COURSE IS GOING 3 TIMES PER WEEK (MWF). MANDATORY ATTENDANCE.

VERY NICE AND APPROACHABLE. You MIGHT ace the exams without the book, but I suggest it because they provide excellent support and a better understanding of the material, ESPECIALLY the introductions to the cases. And when writing papers it helps a lot. PAY ATTENTION in lecture, and read the intros in the book and you'll ace the class

One of the best professors I ever had in college. She made the material that could've been a lot harder to grasp if another professor was teaching it. Probably because she explained ideas and cases well that a middle schooler could understand. Lots of extra credit. About a total of 15 pages of writing broken into seperate parts, but VERY DOABLE.

Very thorough, laid-back and entertaining lectures with plenty of out of class materials, including oral excerpts, youtube videos and other such things. Classwork is relatively easy and the professor is very understanding if issues do arise and quick to help in those circumstances. Highly suggested!
Professor was knowledgeable and entertaining.
more like Jennie Triple Bogey imo
she thinks on a kindergardeners level... and teaches the class the same way. super nice just not bright enough to teach law school
The real-life examples from this professor's legal practice were helpful and interesting. Good class.

I rarely leave reviews, but she was such a poor Professor. No matter how many times u went to office hours, it didn't matter if u were not a male who schmoozed her up. I learned nothing and her feedback was so mean that she sent a separate email telling us "she should have probably been nicer" Glad she retired. Bye Janice, u will not be missed.

The only requirement for doing well in this class is being a hot male. She loves the boys, openly flirts with them in class, and makes sure she caters her lectures to them. Otherwise, she couldnt care less about you or your education. Its like being in public high school all over again. Good luck, ladies (and ugly gents).

Professor Pea is by far one of the best writing professors. She really takes the time to help you out if you have questions. Learning to do legal writing is daunting, and she breaks things down very well.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS

reviews.comment: If you don't like Prof. Pea, it is probably because she gives longer assignments than the other professors. If you can't handle a 3,500 brief in law school, you shouldn't be a lawyer. If you complain about a prof being demanding, can't wait for a partner to review you some day. She is like a mom away from home, too. Love the desserts.
Makes the class needlessly hard and demands too much from her assignments.

Pea is tough, but you are lucky if your section gets her for legal writing. She demands a lot from students but gives a lot of feedback. Make sure to go to office hours as much as possible and ask specific questions!

Professor Pea is one of the best writing professors you could ask for at Illinois. You know she is offering you good advice since this is what she does for a living, essentially writing the law. She expects a lot from her students, but this is all for your benefit. Make the most out of your time with her, it will be well worth it.
Definitely plays favorites and no one has any idea how do do the assignments. If you plan on understanding the assignment you will constantly need to go in for office hours.

No teaching going on as far as what we are actually supposed to do. It was only after the fact through trial and error that we learned about the expectations for our papers. This teacher tends to favor the students that suck up to her.
This class is the most unpleasant part of law school. The material is fine, but compared to other teachers for this class we have twice the amount of work, with half the instruction.
Family Law with Prof. Pea was pleasant. She knew the material, she taught it well, and she graded fairly. Highly recommended.

Just awful in every single way. Doesn't know the material and has no interest in teaching out side of bragging about herself. Also likes to talk about drug use in class, as if we're supposed to believe she smokes pot and that somehow if she did that would make her cool.
I hear she's good for other things, but evidence with Pea is one of the worst classes I have ever had in my life.
Very knowledgeable. Keeps your attention.

Prof. Pea is a good professor, but I felt like she was very interested in students who spent a lot of effort sucking up to her, and had no interest in students who didn't suck up but still had questions. Also, I think it's bad form that she gives out a take home & in-class exam for her classes-it's like having an extra class during exam period.

Prof. Pea is a great teacher.  I really feel that I understand all the concepts she was trying to teach and she uses great stories to illustrate.  She was very friendly and approachable.  Definitely take a class with her if you can.  I would take another class with her if they weren't at night.
Professor Pea is fantastic!  She is easy to understand, and has a fun anecdote for every occasion!  I would definitely take another class with her.

Ross certainly knows her stuff, only wish she had any idea of how to convey it. She reads from a prepared script, moving a mile-a-minute quoting rules of evidence by their numbers. No joke, she actually has a written script. Cold calls, but doesn't know anyone. Began skipping most classes & studied a review book instead- did well on final anyway.

Prof. Ross has been my favorite law professor, so far.  She's passionate and down to Earth. Her lectures are as interesting as they are informative.  The book is clear and comprehensive (basically covers everything she teaches in class). Each chapter contains several hypos that she goes over in class. Been called on twice this month; still not sour

Just random grading. A lot of us that got As in every other class got Bs and B+s. The final was terribly written. When asked to go over the exam, she just sent over a mock answer instead of meeting and explaining why she gave the grade she got.
she seemed pretty scatterbrained.  very nice and intelligent, but not the best professor
Truly the worst professor I've ever had.

Had Ross for 1L Crim Law as a visiting prof. She was disorganized, confused the law at times and basically just read the book out loud. She even used the hypos in class that we read for the day and pretended she just came up with it on the spur of the moment. Thank god for Dressler and his outline.

Not the quality of teacher that U of I requires.  She does forget names, she starts hypo's and frequently tells students they are wrong because of facts she forgot to mention, and often loses track of where we are.  She also seems to choose 3 people to constantly call on and unlike other Professors this is not due to the quality of their answers.
She keeps you on your toes but is very consistent and organized. She uses the cases to illustrate the rules and if you pay attention you'll have no problems on the final.
A policy question on every exam.

Prof. Ross is OOC.  She goes way too fast in class, assigns too much reading, and is TOTALLY AND COMPLETELY disorganized in the way she teaches.  No one in my class ever had any idea what was going on.

Professor Ross is clearly smart, but a very poor teacher.  She did not explain concepts well, did not know the names of the students in her class, refused to discuss the exam until very late in the semester, wrote a dreadful exam which tested very little of what she taught, and refused to allow access to the Rules of Evidence during the exam.

There are far better teachers to take this with.  She really prepares you for nothing, and who does not let you use the rules of evidence on an exam.  Come on!  Listen to the critics, take someone else, unless you like learning things on own.  P.S.  the video clips get very juvenile after awhile.

Prof. Ross stays on schedule, is organized, and uses film clips to give you a chance to test your understanding of the rules. She provides helpful section ending summaries and has been available every time I had a question. Rather than be cautious of her classes, I'd be cautious of her critics her post little more than a vague unsupported warning.

Not a great class.  She tries hard but the material is dense, and dull.  Also at a certain point it becomes insulting when a professor doesn't know your name.  She has far exceeded that point.  It makes it seem like she doesn't care.
No Comments
Don't be fooled;be cautious, if not, just stay clear...

He has no knowledge of real-life transactions, when you ask him about it, he gets angry and asks you to talk to the TA, who has no work experience and knows less than him. The sessions with the TA were bad, he just read from a note and had no in-depth knowledge of the subject.
He looks tough, but he is so kind deep down.

Prof. Kaplan is a brilliant scholar who translates difficult concepts for students. If you need help or do not understand an issue, he takes time to review with you, and when you are done talking to him, you have the concept down. Next to research and writing, his elder law class is the most practical and useful course I had in law school.

Prof. Kaplan is dedicated to students and he takes time to break down difficult concepts. Tax is a difficult subject, but his teaching style is so engaging that one becomes intrigued by the tax implications of almost everything. One of the best professors at the law school, but definitely the best in my opinion.

Kaplan is great and tries his best to make income tax an understandable subject. As the semester goes on he sometimes assumes we know more than we do and it can lead to some confusion going over problems. He's very accessible for office hours though and friendly even outside of class where he'll greet students in the hall if he sees them
reviews.rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS

reviews.comment: Prof. Kaplan gives an excellent lecture, and he makes us available to students.  He is patient and breaks down concepts in a way students can understand. He should be professor of the year every year.

Elder Law is a fun class, but be warned, the exam is tough. Kaplan lectures nearly the entire class, so it's easy to fall asleep, but on the final, he will toss in facts from the lecture that are NOT in the two textbooks that accompany the class. You don't really need to do the reading-it's super complex and uber-detailed. Exam is 50 MC/90 minutes.

Such a niceentertaining professor that tries so hard to make tax understandable that I actually feel guilty for absolutely hating income tax. Would love to have him in any other subject, fed income tax is just not my thang.
Professor Kaplan is one of the best professors I have ever had. He not only knows the material inside out, but he makes want to come to class and listen. I wish there were more professors like him.
A fantastic professor! He knows the material amazingly well and can explain it so clearly. An extremely nice guy as well. I wish he taught more classes.
By far the best professor at the law school.  He is so helpful and a very nice guy.  He tries his best to engage students in the subject.  I wish I had him for income tax.
Fantastic Professor.  He doesn't call on anyone often, but if he does he expects you did the reading!  All around great Professor and very accessible for students.  A+ for Professor Kaplan.

Professor Kaplan is excellent.  He presents difficult and complicated material with clarity.  I especially enjoy his authoritative understanding of the policy underpinnings (or lack thereof) of the tax code.  He is generally very approachable and willing to spend time discussing material with students.  Best professor I've had so far.
Prof. Kaplan is great!

He makes income tax about as good as its going to be, very clear, helpful, polite, quality person.  But its still income tax, and the exam is hard, though its still the same old 3.2ish curve, so the difficulty shouldn't affect your grade much.

Kaplan is great!! Tax is not all that interesting on its own, but he makes it actually kinda fun. Not very much socratic method, he lectures and goes over problems and examples in class. AND he sings songs to you! How great is that in a law proff?!? You don't see that everyday.
Among the best professors.  His exams are not that easy, but he makes the most difficult material comprehendable.  He is the most helpful professor outside of class I have ever met.

Kaplan is engaging and approachable-quite the comedian;makes fed tax manageable;need to stay on top of material-a lot to cram at end;feel free to ask questions in class;need to KNOW the tax rules for exam
Good teacher.  Very clear and organized and smart.  Tax is tough but he keeps it interesting.  Elder law is more discussion oriented.  He' very available to students.

He can be really funny at times. But I learned little in his class. He goes off on tangents and gets distracted by students' never-ending hypo questions, so the learning in each 75-minute class is probably 15 minutes tops. Fyi...he constantly clears his throat IN the microphone at volume 10000000000/10. Ear plugs, stay in your lane self-teaching

He has done some excellent work, empowerment theory is genuinely so good, but you will spend 45 mins of a 75 min class talking about things which are not the law. When he goes into his eighth tangent and you zone out you will miss the next rule, then he will go on another tangent and you will miss the next rule. This repeats until the end of time.

Avoid at all costs. Missed several classes this semester and kept recycling excuses (flat tire, sick, family issues) and showed up 15-30 minutes late for others. Horrible lecturer, acted as if he didn't know what was on the slides, and had really poor time management in class. The semester was a mess and an awful introduction to Contracts.

Kar is awesome. Really cares about his students and passionate about what he is teaching. He provides a great framework for understanding contracts. Kar uses an on-call list instead of cold calling, something every professor should do.

I took a seminar class with Professor Kar and loved it. He's very encouraging and dedicated to creating a welcome environment for students of all backgrounds, and really got me thinking about the subject matter.
You will be hard-pressed to find a teacher more passionate about a subject, and that really comes across in class and through the lecture.

Professor Kar is a very caring professor who takes time outside of the classroom to have review sessions, which is very helpful. However, his final is closed-book, which is pretty ridiculous and unfair considering the sheer amount of material the course covers. I really think his final should not be closed book.
reviews.rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring--LECTURE HEAVY

reviews.comment: Kar is probably the nicest professor I've had here, its incredibly obvious that he cares about his students and wants them each to succeed. He is very focused on the philosophy behind contract law which means that he sometimes has trouble explaining the actual black letter law. I would recommend taking one of his upper level classes instead.
Empowerment Aficionado! Brilliant legal scholars. Very friendly professor. Has time for everyone.
reviews.rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Get ready to read--Hilarious

reviews.comment: Professor Kar is a good professor who really genuinely wants you to succeed. He is extremely knowledgeable on the subject matter but because of this, he does not explain the basic principles well. Overall, an extremely nice professor who wants you to succeed in his class.

Prof Kar is one of the most intelligent people I have ever encountered in my life. However, the best part of about him is that he sincerely cares about his students and is an extraordinary kind and warm-hearted man. He has even taken time on his weekends in order to have extra review sessions. You will be grateful to be placed in Kar's section.

Prof Kar is one of the most knowledge professors in the building and we have a LOT. His contracts article with Professor Radin (one of the most renowned in the country) just got published in the Harvard Law Review journal. He sincerely cares about his students and possibly one of the nicest humans I have ever met. Go to class and you will be fine.
reviews.rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Hilarious

reviews.comment: Prof Kar is very good and ready to answer questions. He has very good hand out! But as a philosopher he spent some time talking about philosophy which is hard for me. the final exam is closed-book and there's one policy question in the end. I hate policy questions T T

He cares about students and gives a good basis of contract law. He wants you to be ready for his exams and helped students feel well prepared. Although I wish he spent a little less time on facts of cases unrelated to contracts, I think he brought it around and left us with an understanding of what the rules were.

Professor Kar is great. It is intimidating that he is an expert in contract law and will count you as absent if he thinks you are unprepared for class, but if you do the readings you will be fine. It is obvious he really cares about his students. Dont be afraid to ask 'dumb questions, he is really nice about answering questions after class.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Caring

reviews.comment: There's no way around it Contracts is confusing. Kar genuinely cares about his students doing well though and will try and help make sure everyone understands what is going on. He's very receptive to altering his class to what the students need (e.g. he started doing more hypos when we asked) so even if we're confused at first we'll get there.

Kar is an amazing professor who is funny and extremely caring. He always asks students for feedback to ensure that he isn't moving too fast, and makes sure to read the faces of his students to see if he is making sense. Do the reading, show up to class, be prepared to be called on (lots of cold calling), and you will do just fine.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring

reviews.comment: Robin Kar is a genuinely nice professor and can be very helpful during office hours, but he is not the clearest instructor. He often glazed over very difficult concepts like defenses, remedies calculations, and spent way too much time on consideration at the beginning of the class. You do not have to read a single case to do well in his class.

Great personality, and is genuine in trying to provide as much of his time to his students as he can. Substantively however, practically everyone in class struggled. The clarity and organization needs a lot of work. Perhaps less detours into philosophy may help.

We spent entirely too much time on formation. He took over a month to learn our names. Also, he is not very responsive to email. Prior to this course, I had heard negative things about him from 2L and 3Ls, and he fulfilled the hype. He does not care about case names on the exam, and I know several people who made As and Bs by reading Contracts E&E.

Amazing professor - lectures are really interesting and he seems to genuinely care about students opinions/understanding of the material. Hes really passionate about the classes he teaches and that definitely comes through during lecture. Grading is fair. One of the best professors at the law school
bad
A friend highly recommended Professor Keenan.  I am not at all happy with that friend right now.
i had no idea what to expect for the final.  hate that.  This class wasn't much work though -- but he seems more interested in appearing cerebral than presenting interesting material.

I had Professor Keenan in the first semester of 2008.  tyhe class is pretty laid back and not very challenging.  however, be prepared to essentially just hear the textbook repeated back to you in the form of powerpoint slides.  that aspect of the class is frustrating because it makes the class very hard to attend.

I liked him a lot. In class he's very interested in opinion questions, which is good and bad. But he knows the law inside and out, and couldn't be a nicer person. Despite the esoteric discussions, he is all black letter on the exam.
i think he's a spousal hire at illinois.  that's the only explanation i have for why he is a professor.  there is seriously no other reason why he's teaching.

My Professional Responsibility professor wasn't professionally responsible enough to turn in his grades on time.  The class is boring, the Professor even more so.  He may be better in other classes with different material, but this class was awful.

He is very nice, and I enjoyed talking to him outside of class.  However, as a Professor he was seriously awful.  I may be a 2L, so there may be worse Professors on the road that lies ahead for me in law school, but I doubt it.

I really enjoyed Prof. Keenan's class. He worked with those he called on and was respectful of those who commented in class. He brought personal insights to various issues and would use currents events to supplement the casebook. Also is well-liked outside of the classroom by many students.

He didn't answer questions fully, ran circles, didn't seem to understand what students were saying and was generally boring.  Perhaps it was just a clash in teaching style with other profs here (he was visiting from U of I), but his style did not sit well with most students.
Was his first time teaching PR, but did a good job.  Class itself sucks, but PJ did a good job.  Very helpful in answering questions and making sure everyone understands.

Kesan is a great person and very jovial, but his lectures for Civ Pro were very unclear. The amount of confusion in the class before the final was ridiculous. He also comes off as very arrogant and often dismisses the answers of students if he thinks your questions are stupid.
Really nice guy. great prof. one of my favorites at law school.
No Comments
No Comments
good in-class professor, but is rarely available outside of class.

Lash was my favorite professor 1L year. He basically creates your outline for you. He repeats a lot of the same material, which is helpful. Also super helpful in office hours and he genuinely cares about his students. His exam was very fair and he covered the topics he emphasized during the semester-due process, state action, suit against executive
Best professor at the law school. The final was very fair.
Shoved his political opinions down the throats of everyone in class.

Very clear, outlines the material for you and is very engaging in class. He warns you  if he's going to cold call you.   My only complaints are he assigns unrealistically heavy reading 1 day before class and then kind of glosses over it in class. Also he didn't mention anything about the exam until 2 days before. Surprise! Its closed book!

Amazing! Clear in his expectations for understanding the material and makes con law interesting. Spends a lot of time outlining the class. The readings were sufficient, but definitely not excessive. Only drawback is he tends to go slow and not always clear on what readings we will get to in class. A truly smart, interesting man.
Really really bad howdy texas

I am so happy I had Dean Wexler for torts. Her lectures are interesting, she uses current events, and does an excellent job of utilizing pictures with her lectures (this is significant for visual learners and memory). She encouraged us to think for ourselves and provided guidance when it was clear no one had any idea what was going on. 10/10

Professor Wexler was an ok professor. She runs through a lot of hypotheticals in the class. The exam was open-book, but it was very tough. She releases most of her recent exams, but not the exam answers. Exam is very rigorous-two issue-spotters with different types of torts-first was more general and second was focused on product liability.

The best professor I ever had. I enjoyed her lectures more than any class I ever had. She is smart, beautiful, and very nice person. Yes she asked a lot of questions to the students but at the end of the lecture you will gain a great amount of knowledge. I can't believe she is so young and she is able to do it. She is super smart.
She's tough, but super nice and funny. She'll take the time to answer your questions and meet with you. You better read though... AND DON'T BE LATE

Makes a fairly straightforward topic confusing- only class I needed a supplement. She does try with her pop culture references, but even that became predictable and boring. If you volunteer she will stick with you for the class, which really hurts classroom discussion. She means well, but no contest - the worst class and professor I had this year.
She is awesome. And beautiful. And smart.
Takes a more comprehensive approach to torts. Buckle your seat belt and get ready to take a lot of notes.

Professor Wexler is a really good teacher who takes the time to make sure you understand the material. She gives a review of the previous class at the beginning of class which was really helpful for outlining. The exam was also very fair and she really prepares you for the content. Be sure to read the note sections at the end of the cases!

Lawless is the goat. Bankruptcy is extremely difficult, but I find myself enjoying the class. Hes made the bankruptcy field accessible by recording short summaries of each chapter and being really accessible (through both our discussion board and office hours). Hes clearly passionate about the topic, and that helps in paying attention in class.

Professor Lawless is very friendly and he is always willing to help students with questions either after class or during office hours. His lectures contain a lot of practical materials that help us understand how the topic actually works in practice. Half of the lecture will consist of doing problem sets. Definitely loved the class!

Prof. Lawless does expect a lot form his students-to do the reading, attend the lectures and participate in class.  But he gives more than  that in return and makes a real effort to know all of his students.  No one is more helpful on this faculty.

Professor Lawless is a smart man, but can't teach very well at all.  He assigns tons of cases that he never talks about, then he expects almost expert-level knowledge of these very poorly written code section while delving into difficult problems that he explains way too quickly.  Two classes like this would be more work than a full-time job.

I liked Reynolds a lot. She is super-energetic, and passionate. However, I think she spends way too much time talking about policy, even though her exam is pure black-letter law. I had a terrific TA when I took Property and she laid out the analysis for the different situations, or else I would not have passed her class. She provides many old exams
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Inspirational--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS

reviews.comment: Most people adore Professor Reynolds, she's extremely kind, passionate about what she does, and is always willing to help students. Unfortunately her teaching style just didn't work for me. It was tough to take good notes, and sometimes she speaks so fast you can't process what she's saying. Exam is closed book.
This woman is awesome! She's bubbly, energetic, fun and at her age she still gets excited about the law and comments students make.
She knows what she's talking about, and she's very helpful.

Fantastic Prof!  She doesn't spoon feed you black letter(HER casebook does that).  In class it's all discussion, but she guides it carefully in the right direction.  Take one of her classes before graduating (State & Local, or Land Use Planning), you'll be glad you did!
High energy class, easy to enjoy despite it being dry subject material.

High energy, but often lets the class ramble and does nothing to tie things together into a clear lesson.  Surprisingly unclear considering she wrote the book she assigns for State & Local Government.
Extremely helpful both in and out of class. Explains things clearly and usually keeps class well on track. Tells you everything you need to know for the exam in class.
Highly excitable and energetic. Wow, does she love property. Fun class and sweet lady. Cute as a button in her LL Bean turtlenecks and clogs.
Really enthusiastic about the material. Tends to often tell class to step it up when she is not satisfied with answers people give her in class. Very helpful outside of class.
Very good at explaining difficult or complicated topics. High energy at a first thing in the morning class is a great bonus. Keeps material tied together very well.

Very high energy and is incredibly willing to talk with students.  Seems to have a second office at IMPE where she will hold discussions while exercising.  Does a good job of explaining difficult concepts.

Best professor ever! Takes a little getting used to because he comes off a little strange at first. It becomes an endearing quality. He actually wants his students to achieve and is not dismissive. It has been a real pleasure having him as my professor
He is an awful teacher. Doesn't allow students to ask questions in class. Has bouts of brilliance but most of the time students have no idea what is going on. Avoid him at all costs!

Professor Solum is an excellent example of how you need a fun, smart professor teaching a dull, difficult course.  He obviously loves teaching, he is frighteningly familiar with the Federal Rules and Statutes, and is very good at clarifying complex matters.  I would take him again the first chance I get.
Good classes, understood what I needed to understand
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--So many papers--Respected

reviews.comment: Unclear, arbitrary, and capricious grading style. It is extremely difficult to figure out what she wants because her feedback is all questions instead of direct statements. She also has a strict word count, making it difficult (if not impossible) to incorporate all of the things she complains about in her feedback. These are 100% unaltered facts.
reviews.rating_tags: Graded by few things--Lecture heavy--Tough grader

reviews.comment: She is awesome. I love her!

She is awesome. As the director of legal writing Moritz knows her stuff. She can be distant and formal. However, she really is a sweet and kind woman if you get to know her. Her office hours are not very helpful. She will see mistakes during office hours & not tell you. Your best bet is to utilize her TAs, and to get proof reading & get advice.

Attendance is mandatory because no one would ever go to this class of their own free will. Don't bother with the reading, there's no need. Good luck trying to pin down what she actually wants from you as she is not very helpful in the mandatory conferences. Above all, don't try to be creative.

Professor Moritz is very intelligent and explains things very well.  I feel that my writing has greatly improved since being in her class.  She wants her students to become better legal writers, and she truly cares about her students.

Professor Moritz is the best! She is always available to meet with you and really does care about how well you do in the course and teaching you. She also teaches in the clearest manner. Everything you need to know, she gives you.

Seems nice but beware.  If she thinks you aren't taking her seriously for some imagined reason, she will slam you.  Buys into the whole law school as 1L boot camp thing.  Teaches you things but a bit threatened and distant.
No Comments
Professor McDade keeps you engaged (and loves football)
reviews.rating_tags: Participation matters--Amazing lectures --Beware of pop quizzes

reviews.comment: Professor McDade is an inspiring professor who helped me love the art of writing. He brings an air of serious academic work to what is usually a mechanical professional education. His lay writing class would better be titled Legal Journalism but is well worth the credits.

I didn't take his class back in 2012, but if what that review says is true, it's definitely changed. Professor McDade has a calm and collected attitude towards everything and is very helpful if you don't understand a concept at first. The readings for Cultural Property Law was very interesting and he makes sure to present good slideshows for them.

Doesn't seem terribly interested in lecturing, but very helpful if you have a specific question or topic you want to work on. Keeps things as lighthearted as possible, given the incredibly boring material that has to be covered.

Professor Maggs is unmatched. It is a privilege to be in his class. His brilliance, kindness, and work ethic inspire me. I enjoy his in-class legal trivial which is very helpful for real-world practice and sheds light on the depth of his knowledge. I am grateful that he teaches at U of I. He definitely could have taught at any Ivy leagues.

I should have come to his office hours more often.  I was having some personal problems in my life and felt like I let him down by not trying hard enough in his class.  He was the main reason I  decided to come to the College of Law.
Last Stand is amazing. He is a genius. He's very old school, but glad I got to have him as a professor. Tough grader, but very fair.
Brilliant guy.  A little difficult to approach after class.  Grades fairly and objectively.  Probably should have taught at a better school.
Last stand is amazing!

Fantastic prof. It takes a while before I understood his teaching style enough to learn from his class. However once I got over that hump the class was hugely entertaining. HIs little asides and tangents on the coursework are worth paying attention to, you'll pick up some random knowledge or laugh.

Socially stunted, but absolutely amazing as a teacher- if you make the effort to contact him.  You have to understand how he thinks though. If you understand how he thinks, the class becomes crystal clear.  It is the getting there that is the hard part.  I half-understood him by the end of contracts, but after trademark, I was guessing his exam Q's

Professor Maggs is an unmatched scholar of law, but this is a rating of Maggs as a teacher.  As a teacher, he's still committed to using a textbook that is incomprehensible and disorganized in its current edition.  Like the textbook author, Maggs disproportionately teaches from the restatement.  Although he's a bit offputting, use his office hours.
The man is a machine.  He knows everything.  Class was organize and socratic, but not stressful and scary.  He's a little awkward and intimidating in person, but a great prof.

I thought he was awful.  While it is true that he is extremely knowledgeable on the subject, Maggs was extremely evasive when you attempted to ask him questions.  It shouldn't be the job of the student to get professors to "open up" before they help you, as other students have suggested.  He seemed either disinterested in teaching.

Great teacher and interesting man. Totally true about all the teaching comments there. Just add something social. We invited him to lunch, and had to make conversations ourselves. If you don't ask questions, he won't say anything voluntarily. He had really intersting experiences. And since the lunch, he'll say hello when I ran into him in school.
uses lots of hypos and calls on people randomly.  VERY knowledgeable on the subject and a really nice guy.  is also very willing to help students with outside of class projects.

He explains the material very well, and makes class fairly entertaining.  He is extremely bizzare in social situations, but probably doesn't mean any harm.  If you like Contract law, you'll probably be happy with him, not that you have a choice.
Maggs is funny, in a dry sort of way, and really has a very interesting class.

A scary, scary man.  Why?  Because he is so much more intelligent than anyone else you will ever meet that he is almost super-human.  He is knows contract law inside and out and is wonderfully generous in sharing that knowledge with his students.  His class was tough, but a privlege.

Maggs is the best!  He is very helpful if you seek him out.  He teaches socratic all the time.  He is brilliant and his classes are organized beyond belief.  It is worth it just for Maggs' last stand (when he recites every case of the semester from Contracts in the last class (50 minutes) by memory).
hard but funny and caring deep inside.  very deep.
Good teacher, hard class.  You will have to fight through the material, but it is worth it.  Fair, objective grader.  The man is brilliant.
People either love, or hate, Maggs.  I ended up liking him.  Be prepared for class, and you will do fine.  Don't expect him to try to be your best friend, he has a strange set of social skills.

An acquired taste--didn't like him at first but he grew on me. Very funny at times. Good teacher for students who are very interested in material and law in general. Great through e-mail, but impossible to talk to one-on-one. Is very fair to students.

Maggs is brilliant;attitude toward students is deplorable-don't look for a hello or smile;need to be assertive w/ Maggs-go to office and e-mail;read and be prepared;taking old exams helpful;you have to MAKE Maggs teach
Super smart guy but class was way too unclear.
His lectures are totally incomprehensible. He made simple concepts 30x more confusing than they actually were. Really did not enjoy this class one bit.

Moore advocates torture.  He advises the CIA on how to implement torture.  He brags about it, just like he brags about his friends in the CIA who ran drugs through Air American and Iran Contra.  He belongs with Prof. Yoo at Stanford and Prof. Goldsmith at Harvard as one of the foremost advocates of the current status quo regarding Guantanamo, etc.
One piece of advice:  use the textbook to clarify and review.  (Crim law)

i left this class feeling ill on many occasions just because I didn't understand the lectures.  I did all of the reading and did the work, but Moore was on a level way above me.  He seemed to teach torts from the more obscure aspects and never really covered the basic information.

I can understand why people find his class to be confusing, but I refuse to call his exam arbitray - torts is a broad subject, and his exam covered the vast majority of what we learned, and I feel my results reflected my understanding of the material...I smell C's, shot egos, and denial

So his tests are arbitrary, so you'll find yourself actually unable to understand his thoughts at times...probably the best and most reputable scholar the law school has, and you should feel grateful he comes down from on high to teach you anything.  if you're into ethics, he's your man. if you have a question, he will answer it...don't be scurred.
Very confusing, not at all helpful and his exams are total crap-shoots
Professor Moore is smarter than me, and I cannot understand him.  However, the man is really very funny and I enjoyed his class, though I spent the whole time completely lost.

Very intelligent professor but he is so smart he's all over the place, and will intellectually dominate you with his long-winded sentences filled with complicated words.  My advice: bring a stenographer to class with you.
Genius at law and wants you to know it.  More interested in this than actually teaching you anything.  Leaves everyone behind.  Traditionally has the lowest grades vs. his peers.
The man is smarter then most, he knows it, and will help you on your way to getting there.  Don't argue with him, lest ye be wanting the academic slap across your 1L dome

I took Prof. Nicola Sharpe's Counsel Practicum, which is two weeks after finals. Class was tough, both because of its timing and Professor Sharpe. Since it's accelerated, class is everyday and the work is heavy. Prof Sharpe is excellent. She is a trove of knowledge and an incredibly educated and wicked smart person. Highly recommend
Great time
Terrific professor, very engaged with the materials, presents the information in a clear and easy to understand manner, always available to discuss cases

Conflated almost every issue. Teaching style can be considered disorganized at best. Has a difficult time distinguishing issues and gets caught up in trivial details and side issues that are not relevant to the class (she spends more time on material unimportant to the class). Showing up and paying attention has a detrimental effect.
Had him at Loyola Chicago. Terrible teacher. Very smart, but cannot communicate the subject matter in a comprehensive way. Buy the hornbook because you wont learn anything in class.
He is a super cool, funny man but a HORRIBLE teacher, in my opinion! I didn't learn a damn thing and barely passed with a supplement. I hated the class and would never take anything with him again.
Understands how the system works. Realizes that the world is full of @#$%.

Excellent professor.  Rambles a bit, but is entertaining when he does.  Buy his hornbook, use it even after Con Law I, because it is well written and concise.  He takes a complicated topic and makes it simple and easy to understand.  His class lectures are key to his exam though.  Make sure you get the hypotheticals down.

Studying for con law was the most stressful experience of my 1L year.  Nowak is a nice guy and entertaining in lecture, but often strays off topic and can be difficult to follow.  Overall though, he is charming and seems to have a kind heart.

I love the guy, but class was confusing and dull (though he knows this and is very realistic about it).  The hornbook is essentially a more orgainized and easy to follow version of his class and is a must if you are having trouble following the lecture.  Grab the concise hornbook and you can teach yourself the entire semester in an afternoon.

Great Professor.  Just buy his hornbook, sit back, and enjoy the show.  You only need to write down a few things during class, don't try to follow every word because you can't.  Just sit back and enjoy the show....

He's a very high quality person, but I can't say I enjoyed his lectures.  He was very hard to follow, and I couldn't stay interested.  He's brilliant, and he may be your taste, but I would not reccomend him.

Nothing wrong with Nowak, he's a funny guy, but he tends to ramble and is hard to follow.  He randomly interjects comments about pointless things into his lectures and goes off on tangents so that it tends to be very hard to take notes in his class.  I would recommend avoiding his classes if possible.

Professor Nowlan is very hard to follow.  He rambles on and on about his grandkids, his ties, and his lawschool classmates...everything but Con Law.  Very enjoyable and entertaining, but you'll have to learn on your own.
He is the best prof at the college of law and probably the coolest as well.

Great guy--funny as hell--but often hard to follow. He skips around a bit but in the end it seems to come together. Definitely look at the horn book or a study aid. His tests aren't bad but the instructions are longer than the questions.

Nowak is very funny;presentations are not clear-expect to be confused;doesn't respond to e-mails; will answer q's in class and after class;grab a good study aid for this guy-you'll be teaching some of this to yourself.

Professor gave amazing lectures. If you are interested in Transportation Engineering, please do not miss this course! The homework and midterm are intense but helpful. And the group projects are very interesting.

Agree, he's a good story teller, but not a good instructor for teaching. He does tried a lot to explain his lecture, but still his explanation is confuse. The point is easy, but he just explained in a complex way. I spend over 6 hours on each of his hw. He uses a wordy way in hw problem and makes it burry and confuse. I don't recommend his class.

He's a good storyteller, but not clear on describing hw. The points that he taught on class does not have many examples to support. I'm struggling with hw every time after class. It's more useful to read the textbook instead of attending his class.
reviews.rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Get ready to read--Lecture heavy

reviews.comment: Great lectures, and interesting topics covered that you won't get anywhere else. The homeworks can be intense, and there isn't much help. You may easily fail a homework, which can be trouble when there are only five. You must go to office hours for the projects, or you will do poorly.

Enthusiastic about the subject. Approachable and will gladly take the time to go over concepts with students, either when asked in class or individually during office hours.  Unfortunately the tests are long and difficult, with very little homework practice/feedback to prepare you.
He is able to teach you and answer to your questions with clarity and patience. Even though the tests and homework may take too much of your time, you end up learning the subject.
He is good.

The grade scale provided is unfair for Professor Stancil. He may be unclear, unhelpful at office hours, and an extremely hard professor. however he is AMAZING and will CHANGE YOUR LIFE. Best professor ever.

He is definitely more enjoyable outside of class. Pretty condescending in class. Always looking to show how smart he is. His final is ridiculous- wanted deep discussion of topics we skimmed over in class. Nice guy out of class, just not a good teacher.

As nice of a guy as there is.  If you get a chance to get to know him, you will not regret it.  He really cares about the students.  I enjoyed his class and would take another from him if I get a chance.

Agree with the comments below, though it's clear he's taught more classes recently.  Great guy, odd sensibilities and sense of humor, and generally very passionate.  I think he could be a great teacher someday.

Good man: clearly wants you to understand the topic but the goal isn't always accomplished. Go to his office hours, listen to others' questions, grab the E&E from the beginning. He wants the minutiae from the casebook, which is a bit odd given the subject but he's testing your reading/comprehension as well. His final feels "gotcha" - very hard.
New to teaching. Safe to say he doesn't know what he's doing, but he'll act like he does. Tends to be condescending. Tries to be funny. Not the worst professor but could be better.

All around great guy. He is a difficult teacher, and if you don't like to read a lot, then you shouldn't take the class. That said, if you want to take a class from a really smart, really friendly, really cool guy and don't mind doing some work, he's your man.

Property is very much a love it or hate it subject, but Ross does a very good job at sparking interest. His class is more policy focused than a lot of other 1L courses, which i found refreshing, and his training as a historian can definintly make you feel like you're in an undergrad history couse at times.
He's distinguished, chill, and surprisingly knows a lot about pop culture. Property law is, in itself, a dull subject, but he tries his best to make it interesting.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Clear grading criteria--Respected

reviews.comment: Ross does well to make a difficult subject like property enjoyable. Has a unique sense of humor and at times I was not 100% convinced he was actually human, but he's a solid professor.

To Ross you are just another number. He does not care about his students and could care less if they pass. He has read from the same outline for years, does not change his hypotheticals, and is here to cash a paycheck. Property could have been a cool subject to learn about but he prevents that in every way. If you fall behind good luck. The worst.

He should not be teaching. While he is very bright, he made it very clear he did not want to answer any questions I had and was incredibly rude and disrespectful to students. While we all can't be as smart as he is, he is here to teach. That is his job.
I hated lectures for property. But, I enjoyed property law itself (i.e., learning it from supplements). I didn't find Ross to be helpful outside of class - he can be very condescending.
Ross is absolutely hilarious. One of my favorite professors so far for sure.

Early Legal Cultures of America is more like an undergrad history seminar than a law school class. The class focuses on the development of early American legal culture. Your grade is based on two 15-page papers (NO bluebook format/extra research outside the reading you need to do for class), and class participation. I found this class to be relaxed
he's hilarious and a great prof.  he really knows how to explain complicated issues, and makes trusts and estates interesting...not an easy task.
Worst reading packet ever. I would suggest not taking Legal History just for that reason.
Good
Legal cultures: very interesting topic and he's done a great job of putitng together good readings.  Class is a good discussion if the other students participate.  Essay grading is lenient.
The class was pretty interesting, the professor was incredibly clear as to what to expect, go to class, do the readings, and you'll be just fine

This class wasn't much work -- you don't really have  to do the reading, but there is tons of boring discussion and the topics as a whole are really boring and repetitive. Plus, I have no idea what to expect for the final.
Doesn't seem to care about her students or her clients. Do not reccomend.
Great professor who cares about her students and wants them to succeed. Highly recommend her!

Prof. Wilson has A LOT going on, at all times. This can make her seem a bit unorganized at times. She is a fair grader and really takes the time to help out students and point them in the right direction. She is pretty transparent with class expectations. She's super nice, just give her a chance and go talk to her sometime if you're struggling!
So unorganized.

Great teacher (I found myself remembering everything she taught us months later), but a little dismissive. She does, however, root for your success. She also doesn't test the Erie doctrine- make of that what you will.

Professor Suja A. Thomas is an incredible professor and a valued asset to the College of Law. Her Employment Discrimination course was my favorite class in law school; I learned so much and she made the materials extremely interesting.
Professor Thomas is extremely smart but she does talk fast. It can be hard to keep up also what she means. That said, she is good intentioned and wants you to succeed.

Professor Thomas is phenomenal. She knows a ton about Civil Procedure, and her lectures are clear. Her powerpoints and sample questions are incredibly helpful. Stay on top of the material and you'll do fine! She's definitely one of the better (best!?) Civ Pro professors. She also publishes extensively and is pretty renowned outside of U of I.

Sports law was a cool class. Every week, there is a speaker who talks to the class about how they became sports lawyers. We had readings each week covering different issues in sports law. Grade determined by two papers, one being an interview with a sports law figure and the other being a research paper. Wish the second paper was due before finals.
reviews.rating_tags: Get ready to read--Participation matters--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS

reviews.comment: I really like Professor Thomas, and she has very organized lecture notes that clearly outline for us the various tests used under different statutes. She constantly encourages class participation, and she offers extremely helpful office hours. She always welcomes students' questions and is very respectful in answering them.

Decent in class, but nothing special. Material translated well to the final, which is a plus. Weird syllabus organization - didn't cover Erie, which is strange. Really poor in office hours - like... really, really poor. Exam review was the least helpful of all the exam reviews I've ever done. You just read your exam yourself and get no feedback.

Super easy class because it is super "obvious" material. Grading rubrics are very hard to understand and she changes her criteria on the fly, at least she is easy to reach out to and get a hold of. It is clear that she has her favorite students.

One of the most passive aggressive people I have met and one of the worst professors at the school. It is extremely tough to talk to her without her lashing out. She doesn't want any technology in her classroom meaning you can't even take notes on your laptop. Save yourself the time and effort and stay away from this class/professor.

Gives a lot of attitude when people don't understand the material/ deliverables. She gets very defensive when we try to give constructive criticisms about her teaching style. The classes are not very organized and she doesn't engage the class too much. The assignments are too confusing to understand because she doesn't explain what needs/wants.

Very vague with project briefs and then has much different expectations in the end, doesn't seem to handle constructive criticism (towards her) well/gets defensive as a result, does not do a good job in engaging students during class, and project brief layouts are confusing and don't accurately depict what she "truly means/wants."
Cryptic and hard to understand when it comes to deliverables. Will be really smug when people are not delivering and confused. Prides herself on trying to outsmart her students.

She's patient and I feel indebted, I went through depression this semester and she was understanding when I told her because I didn't do too well. She gives great advice and is very accessible outside of class. I had her last semester too and I think our entire class can agree that she's good. Sometimes she's unclear but she'll explain if u ask
Great class, I love this professor and would take any class he offered in the future.
The best professor in school; a real academic and a person that loves knowledge.
The smartest guy in school, pleasure to listen to him
exceptional professor
All around great prof, especially if you like economics and want someone who takes it seriously. Brilliant and very much an academic.
genuinely passionate about the relation between law and economics.  laid-back teaching style, very friendly.

Ulen loves classes that seem more like a conversation than a lecture. Unfortunately, the class doesn't always engage. That's ok, his lectures aren't bad. If there's a makeup needed, he buys pizzas for the occasion. Genuinely loves teaching. Don't get excited for this no-curve nonsense - it ends up being a pretty brutal distribution.

Professor Winship was a solid professor. She did a decent job of explaining a subject as confusing as civil procedure. Some notes for future students of this class: 
- Always have a legal backup for your position ready (iykyk)
- Go through practice questions every week 
- Go to her office hours
- Read E&E 
- Rich Freer is your best friend

While I had heard good things about Prof. Winship's Civ Pro class, this was the worst class I've taken at law school. Prof. Winship had little organization to the course, little ability to communicate concepts, and little ability to manage a classroom. Class time was wasted. It wasn't overly difficult since graded on a curve and everyone was lost.
Not an ideal class, rushed through too many topics without much depth which made it seem almost counterintuitive from a student perspective.

Not available after class. Could care less about making a connection with her students. Really lacks an actual understanding of civil procedure and it was obvious. Not enthusiastic about the subject. Wouldnt take her again. Final was easy.

Winship is one of the best profs I've ever had; Civil Procedure is a class that has the potential to be quite dry and complicated, but she made it interesting and understandable. She commands respect while delivering effective and interesting lectures. Very helpful in office hours and outside of class as well. Hope to take another class with her.

WInship is respected by every student in the class. She does a good job of trying to explain confusing things out to you, but she's also not going to coddle you if you get the answer wrong. She doesn't have a problem just flat out saying your wrong and it's really refreshing.

Business Associations is a very boring class, but Winship tries her best to make it interesting. She teaches it like a 1L class going over the rule, facts of the case, and holding carefully.  She also provides practice problems that you go over with a group of students that you are assigned to. Exam was ID and issue spotters. It was a fair exam.

Securities Lit was one of my favorite classes. The material is quite interesting and the reading and workload is relatively light. We had three guest lectures, including from a former SEC enforcement attorney. You have to write a six-page double-spaced paper and give a presentation on a recent topic involving securities. Exam was 1.5 hours long.

Verity was an awesome civpro professor. She breaks down the cases fairly well and gives lots of examples and explanations on how to apply procedure. Probably the funniest law prof I've had; she has a quick wit and charm to her. She's not above lightly shaming gunners which makes her awesome in my book. She's also got a rockin' bod.
Hard but fair

Enjoyed this class and professor a lot. Not always very clear about answers though, supplemental reading was helpful. Fairly organized, practice problems were very helpful as were office hours. Would sign up for another class with her.
She works really hard to make an impossible subject approachable.

Best professor I've had in college. Genuinely wants students to succeed in his class. Attendance not mandatory, but he makes note of who consistently attends class. His knowledge of subject matter and past experiences make the class extremely beneficial. Lecture is laid back and he promotes discussion. Not an easy class, but you will learn a ton.
Very helpful and willing to do anything to help students succeed. Not an easy course, but he is willing to help you get an A.
The class was huge and prompted a closed book exam - which was a bit of an end of semester surprise.  The exam was as easy as they come and I learned a lot more than I realized.

Prof. McDonald is a very nice guy, but he's a terrible professor. Doesn't teach during class periods, just says that he likes what the book says.  Exam is straight forward, but it's so broad and general that it's difficult to know exactly what he's looking for.

The lectures were extremely unhelpful - I had to teach real estate law to myself.  Instead of discussing RE law, he mostly discussed personal anecdotes (how he tore up his neighbors flowers to avoid adverse possession) and told stories about his family (they bought a farm, like to plant trees, etc...).  AVOID THIS PROFESSOR

Really wonderful guy, but class was pretty dull as it was all lecture and he tends to get off track.  Exam was very straightforward, no crazy fact patterns, just simple questions requiring simple answers.
extremely easy going and friendly, good professor who offers solid practical knowledge because he is more concerned with real-life situations than concepts that will never arise in the real world.
I felt like I had to teach myself real estate law because of his disorganization and slighly boring teaching style.  Also, he covered the same topics several times. But he is a jolly guy.
